content:
# Leviticus Chapter 27 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 75r–75v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_027_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_027_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 113–114 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_027_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_027_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 27 is a formally separate appendix to the Holiness Code — indeed to the entire book of Leviticus. Chapters 1–26 constitute the main legislative corpus, crowned by the blessings and curses of chapter 26 which close with the solemn colophon "These are the statutes and judgments and torot that the LORD gave between himself and the sons of Yisra'el on Mount Sinai by the hand of Moshe" (26:46). Chapter 27 then follows as a supplementary appendix dealing with a distinct and closely related topic: the voluntary dedication of persons, animals, land, and possessions to YHWH through formal vows, and the regulated mechanisms by which such dedications may — or may not — be redeemed with a monetary payment. The theological architecture of the chapter is built on a spectrum of holiness that runs from the merely "holy" (qodesh) to the "most holy" (qodesh-qodashim) to the absolutely irreversible cherem ("devoted thing"), with the tithe (ma'aser) as a standing ordinance of holiness for the whole community. The chapter's key technical vocabulary: (a) neder — "vow," from n-d-r, "to vow/promise to God"; a voluntary commitment that creates a binding legal obligation; (b) erekh (plural: arakhin) — "valuation/assessed value," from '-r-kh, "to arrange/set in order/estimate"; the fixed monetary scale by which vowed persons are redeemed, structured by age and sex; these are not market prices for persons but fixed cultic redemption rates; (c) sheqel ha-qodesh — "the sheqel of the sanctuary," the standard unit of weight/currency maintained at the sanctuary; (d) cherem — "devoted thing/banned thing," from ch-r-m, "to devote/ban/put under sacred prohibition"; the most extreme form of dedication, irrevocable and irredeemable; what is placed under cherem belongs to YHWH absolutely and cannot revert to human ownership — it is either destroyed (as with Canaanite cities) or given permanently to the priests; (e) ma'aser — "tithe," from '-s-r, "to take a tenth"; every tenth of agricultural and pastoral produce that belongs inherently to YHWH; (f) yovel — the Jubilee, which sets the framework for valuing dedicated land (vv.17–25), connecting this appendix directly to the Jubilee legislation of chapter 25. The chapter ends with the most solemn colophon in the entire book (v.34): elleh ha-mitsvot asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh el-benei Yisra'el be-har Sinai — "These are the commandments that the LORD commanded Moshe for the sons of Yisra'el on Mount Sinai." This closing formula is broader and more comprehensive than the one at 26:46 (which used torot and statutes and judgments) and crowns the entire book: everything from the first burnt offering to the final tithe regulation is enclosed within the authority of Sinai.]*

---

## Leviticus 27

**27:1** And the LORD spoke to Moshe [va-yedabber YHWH el-Moshe — וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה; the standard prophetic formula opening a new legislative section; this is the final such formula in the book of Leviticus — all that follows is the last word YHWH will speak through Moshe before the book closes], saying:

**27:2** Speak to the sons of Yisra'el [dabber el-benei Yisra'el — דַּבֵּר אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל] and say to them [ve-amarta alehem — וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵהֶם]: When a man makes a special vow [ish ki yafli neder — אִישׁ כִּי יַפְלִא נֶדֶר; ki yafli — Hifil imperfect of p-l-', "to be extraordinary/to do something extraordinary/to be difficult/to be wonderful"; the Hifil yafli — "makes extraordinary/makes difficult/does something extraordinary/makes a specific/special vow" — this is not the ordinary neder (vow) but a particularly solemn or elaborate vow involving the dedication of persons; the root p-l-' always carries a connotation of the extraordinary — it is the root of pele' ("wonder/marvel"), and yafli neder designates a vow of extraordinary or special character, typically involving dedication of persons to God; LXX: ean de apotelese euchen — "if he fulfills/accomplishes a vow"], according to your valuation of persons to the LORD [be-erkekha nefashot la-YHWH — בְּעֶרְכְּךָ נְפָשׁוֹת לַיהוָה; be-erkekha — "by your valuation/by your assessed value," with the second-person singular suffix addressed to the priest who performs the assessment; erkkha — "your valuation," from '-r-kh, "to arrange/set/assess"; erekh is the technical term for the fixed monetary scale that follows; nefashot — "persons/souls/lives," from nefesh, "breath/life/person/self"; the "valuation of persons" — not the person's market value as a slave but the official sanctuary rate for redeeming a person who has been vowed to YHWH]:

**27:3** then your valuation shall be [ve-hayah erkekha — וְהָיָה עֶרְכְּךָ]: for a male from twenty years old and up to sixty years old [ha-zakhar mi-ben esrim shanah ve-ad ben-shishim shanah — הַזָּכָר מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וְעַד בֶּן-שִׁשִּׁים שָׁנָה; the prime working years of adult male life, from twenty (the age of military conscription and census in Num 1) to sixty (the age at which labor capacity was considered to decline]; your valuation shall be fifty sheqels of silver [ve-hayah erkekha chamishim sheqel kesef — וְהָיָה עֶרְכְּךָ חֲמִשִּׁים שֶׁקֶל כֶּסֶף; chamishim sheqel kesef — "fifty sheqels of silver"; the sheqel was a unit of weight (approximately 11.4 grams), not a coin; kesef — "silver"; fifty sheqels is the highest valuation in the table — the maximum redemption amount for the most productive demographic] by the sheqel of the sanctuary [be-sheqel ha-qodesh — בְּשֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ; the sheqel ha-qodesh — "the sanctuary sheqel/the holy sheqel" — the standard weight maintained at the sanctuary, ensuring that commercial fluctuation in weights and measures did not affect sacred payments; the sanctuary maintained the standard against which commercial weights were measured].

**27:4** And if she is a female [ve-im neqevah hi — וְאִם-נְקֵבָה הִוא], your valuation shall be thirty sheqels [ve-hayah erkekha sheloshim sheqel — וְהָיָה עֶרְכְּךָ שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁקֶל; thirty sheqels for a woman of the prime age bracket (20–60); this is the same amount paid for Yosef by the Midianites (Gen 37:28) and the figure cited in Zech 11:13 as the "lordly price" of a shepherd — later echoed in the betrayal price of Yeshua; the differential between male and female valuations in the table reflects assessments of labor capacity and is a cultic economic convention, not a statement of ontological worth].

**27:5** And if from five years old and up to twenty years old [ve-im mi-ben chameish shanim ve-ad ben-esrim shanah — וְאִם מִבֶּן חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים וְעַד בֶּן-עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה], your valuation for a male shall be twenty sheqels [ve-hayah erkekha la-zakhar esrim sheqel — וְהָיָה עֶרְכְּךָ לַזָּכָר עֶשְׂרִים שָׁקֶל] and for a female ten sheqels [ve-la-neqevah asarah sheqel — וְלַנְּקֵבָה עֲשָׂרָה שְׁקָלִים; the adolescent/juvenile bracket — ages 5 to 20 — is valued at lower rates than prime adulthood; for this age range a male is 20 sheqels and a female is 10 sheqels].

**27:6** And if from a month old and up to five years old [ve-im mi-ben-chodesh ve-ad ben-chameish shanim — וְאִם מִבֶּן-חֹדֶשׁ וְעַד בֶּן-חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים], your valuation for a male shall be five sheqels of silver [ve-hayah erkekha la-zakhar chamisha sheqalim kesef — וְהָיָה עֶרְכְּךָ לַזָּכָר חֲמִשָּׁה שְׁקָלִים כָּסֶף] and for a female your valuation shall be three sheqels of silver [ve-la-neqevah erkekha sheloshet sheqalim kesef — וְלַנְּקֵבָה עֶרְכְּךָ שְׁלֹשֶׁת שְׁקָלִים כָּסֶף; the youngest children are valued at the minimum rates — 5 sheqels (male) and 3 sheqels (female); the lower bound is one month of age, corresponding to the pidyon ha-ben ("redemption of the firstborn") in Num 18:16 which also sets five sheqels as the redemption price from one month onward].

**27:7** And if from sixty years old and upward [ve-im mi-ben-shishim shanah va-ma'lah — וְאִם מִבֶּן-שִׁשִּׁים שָׁנָה וָמָעְלָה; va-ma'lah — "and upward," the standard phrase for the open-ended upper bracket]: for a male your valuation shall be fifteen sheqels [le-zakhar chamisha asar sheqel — לַזָּכָר חֲמִשָּׁה עָשָׂר שָׁקֶל] and for a female ten sheqels [ve-la-neqevah asarah sheqalim — וְלַנְּקֵבָה עֲשָׂרָה שְׁקָלִים; the elderly bracket (60+) receives lower valuations than prime adulthood — 15 sheqels (male) and 10 sheqels (female); notably the elderly female (10 sheqels) equals the male rate for ages 5–20, and the elderly male (15 sheqels) falls well below the prime adult male rate of 50; the table systematically reflects labor capacity].

**27:8** But if he is too poor [ve-im makh hu — וְאִם-מָךְ הוּא; makh — "poor/low/depressed/reduced," from m-kh-kh, "to be low/brought down/poor"; the egalitarian provision that prevents the valuation system from excluding the economically marginal from fulfilling their vows] for your valuation [me-erkekha — מֵעֶרְכְּךָ; "too poor for/below your assessed value," — me- indicating a fall short of the standard], then he shall stand before the priest [ve-he'emido lifnei ha-kohen — וְהֶעֱמִידוֹ לִפְנֵי הַכֹּהֵן; he'emido — Hifil of '-m-d, "to cause to stand/to present"; the person is brought before the priest for individual assessment — the fixed table gives way to pastoral case-by-case judgment], and the priest shall value him [ve-he'erekho ha-kohen — וְהֶעֱרִיכוֹ הַכֹּהֵן; he'erekho — Hifil of '-r-kh, "to value/assess/set a value upon him"]; according to what the one who vowed can afford [al-pi asher tassig yad ha-noder — עַל-פִּי אֲשֶׁר תַּשִּׂיג יַד הַנֹּדֵר; al-pi — "according to the mouth of/according to"; tassig yad — literally "the hand reaches/attains," idiomatic for financial capacity; "according to what the hand of the one who vowed can reach/afford" — this is the ability-to-pay principle; ha-noder — "the one who made the vow," Qal participle of n-d-r] the priest shall value him [ya'erekhennu ha-kohen — יַעֲרִיכֶנּוּ הַכֹּהֵן; the priest's assessed value replaces the fixed table amount; no Israelite is excluded from the vow system by poverty].

---

**27:9** And if it is from the animals [ve-im behemah — וְאִם-בְּהֵמָה; the law now shifts from persons (vv.2–8) to animals; behemah — domestic livestock; the general category covering both clean and unclean animals, but the sub-cases are distinguished in vv.9–11] from which one brings an offering to the LORD [asher yaqrivu mimmenah qorban la-YHWH — אֲשֶׁר יַקְרִיבוּ מִמֶּנָּה קָרְבָּן לַיהוָה; the clause identifies acceptable-offering animals — cattle, sheep, goats (the animals regularly brought as qorban); yaqrivu — Hifil imperfect of q-r-v, "to bring near/offer/present" — the formal offering verb], all that he gives from it to the LORD shall be holy [kol asher yitten mimmenah la-YHWH yihyeh-qodesh — כֹּל אֲשֶׁר יִתֵּן מִמֶּנָּה לַיהוָה יִהְיֶה-קֹּדֶשׁ; the moment a clean animal is verbally consecrated by vow, it enters the status of qodesh — "holy/set apart"; it cannot be reclaimed, downgraded, or exchanged; the vow creates an irrevocable status].

**27:10** He shall not exchange it [lo yachalifennu — לֹא יַחֲלִיפֶנּוּ; yachalifennu — Hifil imperfect of ch-l-f, "to exchange/replace/substitute"; once vowed, no substitution is permitted] or swap it [ve-lo yamir oto — וְלֹא יָמִיר אֹתוֹ; yamir — Hifil imperfect of m-w-r/m-y-r, "to change/exchange/barter"; two different verbs for exchange (ch-l-f and m-w-r) emphasize the absolute prohibition from every angle] — a good for a bad or a bad for a good [tov be-ra' o ra' be-tov — טוֹב בְּרָע אוֹ-רָע בְּטוֹב; explicitly ruling out even the seemingly innocuous substitution of a better animal for a worse, or vice versa; the vowed animal is fixed in its consecration; even a superior replacement is forbidden because the owner may be motivated by convenience or profit]; and if he does indeed swap [ve-im hamor yamir — וְאִם-הָמֵר יָמִיר; the infinitive absolute hamor intensifying the finite verb yamir — "if he does indeed exchange/if he exchanges exchanging"; the conditional: despite the prohibition, if he does it anyway] animal for animal [behemah bi-vehemah — בְּהֵמָה בִּבְהֵמָה], then it and its substitute shall both be holy [ve-hayah hu u-temurato yihyeh-qodesh — וְהָיָה-הוּא וּתְמוּרָתוֹ יִהְיֶה-קֹּדֶשׁ; temuratah — "its exchange/its substitute," from m-w-r/t-m-r; the penalty for attempted substitution is that both animals become holy — the original consecrated animal and the substitute are both consecrated; the attempt to un-consecrate backfires and doubles the consecration; no human manipulation can reduce what belongs to YHWH].

**27:11** And if it is any unclean animal [ve-im kol-behemah teme'ah — וְאִם כָּל-בְּהֵמָה טְמֵאָה; teme'ah — "ritually unclean/impure," the Qal passive participle of t-m-', "to be unclean"; unclean animals cannot be offered at the altar, so their consecration is expressed differently] of which one does not bring as an offering to the LORD [asher lo-yaqrivu mimmenah qorban la-YHWH — אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יַקְרִיבוּ מִמֶּנָּה קָרְבָּן לַיהוָה; the negative of the clause in v.9 — animals of the type that cannot be brought as altar-offerings], then he shall present the animal before the priest [ve-he'emid et-ha-behemah lifnei ha-kohen — וְהֶעֱמִיד אֶת-הַבְּהֵמָה לִפְנֵי הַכֹּהֵן; the unclean animal, like the poor person in v.8, must be brought to the priest for assessment].

**27:12** And the priest shall value it [ve-he'erekho ha-kohen — וְהֶעֱרִיכוֹ הַכֹּהֵן] — whether it is good or bad [bein tov u-vein ra' — בֵּין טוֹב וּבֵין רָע; the priest exercises judgment about the animal's quality and condition] — according to your valuation, O priest [ke-erkekha ha-kohen — כְּעֶרְכְּךָ הַכֹּהֵן; the priest's assessed value stands; the priest is here addressed directly with the vocative ha-kohen], so it shall be [ken yihyeh — כֵּן יִהְיֶה].

**27:13** But if he redeems it [ve-im ga'ol yig'alennu — וְאִם-גָּאֹל יִגְאָלֶנּוּ; the infinitive absolute ga'ol intensifying the finite verb yig'alennu — "if he does indeed redeem it"; ga'al — "to redeem/act as kinsman-redeemer/buy back"], then he shall add a fifth to your valuation [ve-yasaf chamishito al-erkekha — וְיָסַף חֲמִשִׁיתוֹ עַל-עֶרְכֶּךָ; chamishito — "its fifth/one-fifth of it"; the twenty-percent surcharge (one-fifth = 20%) added to the priest's assessed value; this surcharge recurs throughout the chapter (vv.13, 15, 19, 27, 31) as the standard cost of redemption — the owner pays the assessed value plus 20% to reclaim what was consecrated to YHWH; it discourages casual vow-making and reclamation].

---

**27:14** And a man who consecrates [ve-ish ki yaqddish — וְאִישׁ כִּי יַקְדִּשׁ; yaqddish — Hifil imperfect of q-d-sh, "to make holy/consecrate/dedicate"; the law now turns to the dedication of houses] his house as holy to the LORD [et-beito qodesh la-YHWH — אֶת-בֵּיתוֹ קֹדֶשׁ לַיהוָה; the verbal dedication of a house — "his house" — to YHWH through vow] — the priest shall value it [ve-he'erekho ha-kohen — וְהֶעֱרִיכוֹ הַכֹּהֵן] whether it is good or bad [bein tov u-vein ra' — בֵּין טוֹב וּבֵין רָע]: as the priest values it [ka'asher ya'arekhennu ha-kohen — כַּאֲשֶׁר יַעֲרִיכֶנּוּ הַכֹּהֵן], so it shall stand [ken yaqum — כֵּן יָקוּם; yaqum — Qal imperfect of q-w-m, "to stand/arise/be established"; "as the priest values it, so it shall stand/be established" — the priestly assessment is final and binding].

**27:15** And if the one who consecrated it redeems his house [ve-im ha-maqddish yig'al et-beito — וְאִם-הַמַּקְדִּישׁ יִגְאַל אֶת-בֵּיתוֹ; ha-maqddish — "the one who consecrated," the Hifil participle; yig'al — Qal imperfect of g-'-l, "to redeem"], then he shall add a fifth of the silver of your valuation to it [ve-yasaf chamishit kesef-erkekha alav — וְיָסַף חֲמִישִׁית כֶּסֶף-עֶרְכְּךָ עָלָיו; the same 20% surcharge as in v.13; kesef-erkekha — "the silver of your valuation/the money of your assessed value"], and it shall belong to him [ve-hayah lo — וְהָיָה לוֹ; upon payment of the assessed value plus the fifth, the house reverts to its original owner].

---

**27:16** And if a man consecrates to the LORD some of the field of his holding [ve-im mi-sedeh achuzato yaqddish ish la-YHWH — וְאִם מִשְּׂדֵה אֲחֻזָּתוֹ יַקְדִּשׁ אִישׁ לַיהוָה; sedeh achuzatah — "field of his holding/ancestral holding-field"; achuzah — the inalienable ancestral land-portion (cf. Lev 25:10, 25–28); the Jubilee framework governs the valuation of land dedications], your valuation shall be according to its seed [ve-hayah erkekha le-fi zar'o — וְהָיָה עֶרְכְּךָ לְפִי זַרְעוֹ; le-fi zar'o — "according to the mouth of its seed/proportionate to its seed," i.e., the amount of seed required to sow the field; seed-capacity is the standard of area measurement]: an area sown with a homer of barley seed [zera' omer se'orah — זֶרַע חֹמֶר שְׂעֹרִים; omer — a homer, the largest dry measure, approximately 220 liters; se'orah — "barley"; the homer of barley seed was the unit by which the field's area was measured for valuation purposes] at fifty sheqels of silver [ba-chamishim sheqel kesef — בַּחֲמִשִּׁים שֶׁקֶל כָּסֶף; fifty sheqels for each homer-of-barley's-worth of land area; this is the same as the highest personal valuation (v.3), suggesting an approximate equivalence between a prime male's labor and a field's productive capacity].

**27:17** If from the year of the jubilee he consecrates his field [im mi-shenat ha-yovel yaqddish sadehhu — אִם מִשְּׁנַת הַיּוֹבֵל יַקְדִּישׁ שָׂדֵהוּ], your valuation shall stand [be-erkekha yaqum — בְּעֶרְכְּךָ יָקוּם; at the start of a Jubilee cycle, the full valuation applies — the dedicant loses the full productive period; yaqum — "it shall stand/be established"].

**27:18** But if he consecrates his field after the jubilee [ve-im achar ha-yovel yaqddish sadehhu — וְאִם אַחַר הַיּוֹבֵל יַקְדִּישׁ שָׂדֵהוּ], then the priest shall calculate for him the silver [ve-chishav-lo ha-kohen et-ha-kesef — וְחִשַּׁב-לוֹ הַכֹּהֵן אֶת-הַכֶּסֶף; chishav — Pi'el perfect of ch-sh-v, "to calculate/reckon/count/devise"; the priest performs a pro-rated calculation] according to the remaining years [al-pi ha-shanim ha-notirot — עַל-פִּי הַשָּׁנִים הַנּוֹתָרֹת; ha-notirot — "the remaining/left-over years," Niphal participle of y-t-r, "to remain/be left over"] until the year of the jubilee [ad shenat ha-yovel — עַד שְׁנַת הַיּוֹבֵל], and it shall be deducted from your valuation [ve-nigar me-erkekha — וְנִגְרַע מֵעֶרְכֶּךָ; nigar — Niphal perfect of g-r-', "to be diminished/reduced/deducted"; the full valuation is proportionally reduced based on how many years remain before the Jubilee, when the land would revert regardless].

**27:19** And if the one who consecrated the field redeems it [ve-im ga'ol yig'al et-ha-sadeh ha-maqddish oto — וְאִם-גָּאֹל יִגְאַל אֶת-הַשָּׂדֶה הַמַּקְדִּישׁ אֹתוֹ; the same structure as v.13 with the intensifying infinitive absolute; ha-maqddish — "the one who consecrated it"], then he shall add a fifth of the silver of your valuation to it [ve-yasaf chamishit kesef-erkekha alav — וְיָסַף חֲמִישִׁית כֶּסֶף-עֶרְכְּךָ עָלָיו], and it shall revert to him [ve-qam lo — וְקָם לוֹ; qam lo — "it shall stand/revert to him"; the field is fully restored to the consecrator upon payment of the assessed value plus the 20% surcharge].

**27:20** But if he does not redeem the field [ve-im lo yig'al et-ha-sadeh — וְאִם לֹא יִגְאַל אֶת-הַשָּׂדֶה], or if he has sold the field to another man [ve-im makar et-ha-sadeh le-ish acher — וְאִם-מָכַר אֶת-הַשָּׂדֶה לְאִישׁ אַחֵר; the two failure-scenarios: either the dedicant never redeems it, or he sells it to a third party (making redemption more complicated)], it shall no longer be redeemed [lo yig'al od — לֹא יִגָּאֵל עוֹד; the Niphal of g-'-l — "it shall not be redeemed/be bought back any further"; once the field has been sold to another while consecrated, the original owner's right of redemption is extinguished].

**27:21** And the field, when it goes out in the jubilee [ve-hayah ha-sadeh be-tsetoh va-yovel — וְהָיָה הַשָּׂדֶה בְּצֵאתוֹ בַיֹּבֵל; be-tsetoh — "in its going out/at its release/when it goes forth," the Qal infinitive construct of y-ts-', "to go out/come out/be released"; when the Jubilee year arrives and the field "goes out" of its current holding-arrangement], it shall be holy to the LORD [qodesh la-YHWH yihyeh — קֹדֶשׁ לַיהוָה יִהְיֶה; the unredeemed consecrated field, at Jubilee, becomes permanently holy — it cannot revert to the original owner] as a devoted field [ki-sedeh ha-cherem — כִּשְׂדֵה הַחֵרֶם; ki-sedeh ha-cherem — "like a field under cherem/like a devoted field"; the cherem is introduced here by analogy — the unredeemed dedicated field takes on the status of cherem, the most absolute form of consecration; cherem from ch-r-m, "to devote/ban/place under sacred prohibition"]; it becomes the priest's holding [la-kohen tihyeh achuzato — לַכֹּהֵן תִּהְיֶה אֲחֻזָּתוֹ; achuzatah — "his holding/his possession" — the inalienable ancestral-land term now applied to the priest; what was the original owner's achuzah becomes the priest's achuzah; the field passes permanently to the priestly inheritance].

**27:22** And if he consecrates to the LORD a field that he has bought [ve-im et-sedeh miqnatoh asher lo mi-sedeh achuzato yaqddish la-YHWH — וְאִם אֶת-שְׂדֵה מִקְנָתוֹ אֲשֶׁר לֹא מִשְּׂדֵה אֲחֻזָּתוֹ יַקְדִּישׁ לַיהוָה; sedeh miqnatoh — "a field of his purchase/a purchased field," from q-n-h, "to buy/acquire"; asher lo mi-sedeh achuzato — "which is not from his ancestral holding-field" — a critical distinction: this is not inherited ancestral land but property acquired commercially; the rules for purchased land differ from those for ancestral land]:

**27:23** then the priest shall calculate for him [ve-chishav-lo ha-kohen — וְחִשַּׁב-לוֹ הַכֹּהֵן] the amount of your valuation up to the year of the jubilee [et michsat ha-erakh ad shenat ha-yovel — אֵת מִכְסַת הָעֵרֶךְ עַד שְׁנַת הַיּוֹבֵל; michsat ha-erakh — "the amount of the valuation/the reckoned assessment," from m-k-s, "to apportion/calculate a sum"; the pro-rated value for the remaining years until Jubilee], and he shall give your valuation on that day [ve-natan et-ha-erakh ba-yom ha-hu — וְנָתַן אֶת-הָעֵרֶךְ בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא; payment is due immediately, on that day, unlike inherited land where redemption can be delayed] — it is holy to the LORD [qodesh la-YHWH — קֹדֶשׁ לַיהוָה].

**27:24** In the year of the jubilee the field shall return [bi-shenat ha-yovel yashuv ha-sadeh — בִּשְׁנַת הַיּוֹבֵל יָשׁוּב הַשָּׂדֶה; yashuv — Qal imperfect of sh-w-v, "to return/go back"] to the one from whom he bought it [la'asher qanahu me-itto — לַאֲשֶׁר קָנָהוּ מֵאִתּוֹ; qanahu — "he bought it from him"; the Jubilee's land-reversion principle applies to purchased land: at Jubilee it reverts not to the dedicant (who purchased it and then consecrated it) but to the original owner from whom the dedicant bought it] — to him whose ancestral holding is the land [la'asher lo achuzat ha-aretz — לַאֲשֶׁר-לוֹ אֲחֻזַּת הָאָרֶץ; the phrase emphasizes that the original owner holds the ancestral relationship to the land; the Jubilee restores the original achuzah-structure of the land].

**27:25** And all your valuations shall be by the sheqel of the sanctuary [ve-khal-erkekha yihyeh be-sheqel ha-qodesh — וְכָל-עֶרְכְּךָ יִהְיֶה בְּשֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ; a summary statement applying to all valuations throughout the chapter]: twenty gerahs shall be the sheqel [esrim gerah yihyeh ha-sheqel — עֶשְׂרִים גֵּרָה יִהְיֶה הַשָּׁקֶל; the gerah was the smallest unit of weight — one twentieth of a sheqel, approximately 0.57 grams; the standard exchange rate: 1 sheqel = 20 gerahs; this ensures the sanctuary standard is precisely defined against a smaller unit].

---

**27:26** However, the firstborn of the animals [akh bekhor asher yevukkar la-YHWH — אַךְ בְּכוֹר אֲשֶׁר-יְבֻכַּר לַיהוָה; akh — "however/but/only" — the contrastive particle introducing an exception; bekhor — "firstborn"; yevukkar — Pual imperfect of b-k-r, "to be born first/be the firstborn/be designated firstborn," from b-k-r, "firstborn/early fruit"; the Pual passive: "that is made/designated firstborn to the LORD"] among the animals — no man shall consecrate it [lo yaqddish ish oto — לֹא יַקְדִּישׁ אִישׁ אֹתוֹ]; whether cattle or sheep [im shor im seh — אִם-שׁוֹר אִם-שֶׂה; the two major domesticated offerings: shor — "bull/ox/head of cattle," seh — "one of the flock/sheep or goat"], it belongs to the LORD [la-YHWH hu — לַיהוָה הוּא; the firstborn already belongs to YHWH — this is not a vow but a pre-existing divine claim (cf. Exod 13:2, 12–15; Num 18:17); one cannot consecrate to God what already belongs to God].

**27:27** And if it is among the unclean animals [ve-im ba-behemah ha-teme'ah — וְאִם בַּבְּהֵמָה הַטְּמֵאָה], then he shall buy it back by your valuation [u-fedahu be-erkekha — וּפְדָאוֹ בְעֶרְכֶּךָ; fedahu — Qal perfect of p-d-h, "to ransom/redeem/buy back"; for unclean firstborn animals, which cannot be offered at the altar, the owner may redeem them by paying the assessed value to the sanctuary] and add a fifth to it [ve-yasaf chamishito alav — וְיָסַף חֲמִשִׁיתוֹ עָלָיו; the standard 20% surcharge]; and if it is not redeemed [ve-im lo yig'al — וְאִם לֹא יִגָּאֵל], then it shall be sold by your valuation [ve-nimkar be-erkekha — וְנִמְכַּר בְּעֶרְכֶּךָ; nimkar — Niphal perfect of m-k-r, "to be sold"; the unclean firstborn that is neither redeemed nor eaten is sold at the assessed value, the proceeds going to the sanctuary].

---

**27:28** But any devoted thing [akh kol-cherem — אַךְ כָּל-חֵרֶם; akh — again the contrastive particle marking the absolute exception; cherem — "devoted thing/banned thing," from ch-r-m, "to devote completely/to place under sacred ban"; this is the most extreme category in the chapter: total, irrevocable consecration; the root ch-r-m also underlies the word for the prohibition of a thing, the sacred destruction of enemy cities (cherem warfare), and the total-ban legislation of Deuteronomy] that a man devotes to the LORD [asher yacharem ish la-YHWH — אֲשֶׁר יַחֲרִם אִישׁ לַיהוָה; yacharem — Hifil imperfect of ch-r-m, "to place under cherem/to devote absolutely"] from all that is his [mi-kol asher lo — מִכֹּל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ] — from human and animal [me-adam u-vehemah — מֵאָדָם וּבְהֵמָה; the cherem applies universally to persons and animals dedicated under this absolute form] and from the field of his holding [u-mi-sedeh achuzato — וּמִשְּׂדֵה אֲחֻזָּתוֹ] — shall not be sold [lo yimmaker — לֹא יִמָּכֵר; Niphal imperfect of m-k-r — it shall not be sold to a third party] and shall not be redeemed [ve-lo yig'al — וְלֹא יִגָּאֵל; it cannot be bought back by the original owner]: every devoted thing [kol-cherem — כָּל-חֵרֶם] is most holy [qodesh-qadashim hu — קֹדֶשׁ-קֳדָשִׁים הוּא; qodesh-qadashim — "holy of holies/most holy"; the construct chain intensifying holiness to its absolute degree — the same phrase applied to the incense altar, the showbread, the sin offering, and the innermost sanctum; the cherem object occupies the same sphere of absolute holiness as the Holy of Holies itself] to the LORD [la-YHWH — לַיהוָה].

**27:29** No devoted person [kol-cherem asher yucharam min-ha-adam — כָּל-חֵרֶם אֲשֶׁר יָחֳרַם מִן-הָאָדָם; yucharam — Hophal imperfect of ch-r-m, "to be devoted/to be placed under cherem," the passive form; the verse addresses persons placed under cherem — the most extreme case] shall be redeemed [lo yifdeh — לֹא יִפָּדֶה; Niphal imperfect of p-d-h, "to be ransomed/redeemed"; no ransom payment can release a person placed under absolute cherem; this is in sharp contrast with the ordinary consecration of persons in vv.2–8, which is fully redeemable; the cherem is an absolute, unreversible transfer]; he shall surely be put to death [mot yumat — מוֹת יוּמָת; the infinitive absolute mot intensifying the finite verb yumat — "he shall dying-die/he shall certainly be put to death"; the Hophal of m-w-t — "to be caused to die"; the person under cherem cannot be redeemed and must die; this provision connects to the cherem war-texts where captured enemies are devoted and destroyed; it reflects the absolute nature of the cherem status: complete transfer to YHWH with no human claim remaining].

---

**27:30** And all the tithe of the land [ve-khol ma'aser ha-aretz — וְכָל-מַעְשַׂר הָאָרֶץ; ma'aser — "tithe/tenth," from '-s-r, "to take a tenth/to tithe"; the tithe is now introduced as a separate standing ordinance, not a vow but a regular obligation inherent in the produce of the land] — from the seed of the land [mi-zera' ha-aretz — מִזֶּרַע הָאָרֶץ], from the fruit of the tree [mi-peri ha-etz — מִפְּרִי הָעֵץ] — it belongs to the LORD [la-YHWH hu — לַיהוָה הוּא; the tithe, like the firstborn (v.26), inherently belongs to YHWH — it is not a gift to be given but a debt to be paid back]; it is holy to the LORD [qodesh la-YHWH — קֹדֶשׁ לַיהוָה; the tithe enters qodesh — holy — status by virtue of being the Lord's own portion; not through consecration by the owner but through divine claim].

**27:31** And if a man redeems some of his tithe [ve-im ga'ol yig'al ish mi-ma'asero — וְאִם-גָּאֹל יִגְאַל אִישׁ מִמַּעַשְׂרוֹ; the infinitive absolute intensifying again — "if he does indeed redeem/if he redeems some of his tithe"; the tithe in kind (grain, fruit) may be redeemed in silver if it is impractical to transport the actual produce to the sanctuary], then he shall add a fifth to it [ve-chamishito yosif alav — וַחֲמִישִׁיתוֹ יֹסִיף עָלָיו; the standard 20% surcharge applies here as well; the tithe's monetary redemption costs more than the tithe itself, discouraging casual substitution of money for produce].

**27:32** And of all the tithe of the herd and the flock [ve-khol-ma'aser baqar va-tson — וְכָל-מַעְשַׂר בָּקָר וָצֹאן; the agricultural tithe (v.30) is now followed by the pastoral tithe — every tenth animal; baqar — "cattle/herd animals"; tson — "flock/sheep and goats"] — of all that passes under the rod [kol asher ya'avor tachat ha-shevet — כֹּל אֲשֶׁר יַעֲבֹר תַּחַת הַשָּׁבֶט; ya'avor tachat ha-shevet — "that passes under the rod/staff"; the counting procedure: the animals are driven past the shepherd's rod/staff, and every tenth one is automatically consecrated as it crosses under the rod; tachat ha-shevet — "under the rod" — the physical act of passing under the shepherd's counting rod designates the tithe; shevet — "rod/staff/tribe/scepter"] — the tenth shall be holy to the LORD [ha-asiri yihyeh-qodesh la-YHWH — הָעֲשִׂירִי יִהְיֶה-קֹּדֶשׁ לַיהוָה; ha-asiri — "the tenth/the tithe-one," the ordinal; whichever animal is the tenth as they pass under the rod becomes holy to YHWH automatically, regardless of its quality].

**27:33** He shall not look to see [lo yevaqer — לֹא יְבַקֵּר; yevaqer — Pi'el imperfect of b-q-r, "to inspect/examine/look carefully/discriminate"; the owner is prohibited from inspecting the animals before they pass under the rod to select which ones will be counted as the tenth] whether it is good or bad [bein tov le-ra — בֵּין-טוֹב לָרַע; the selection is random, driven by the mechanical count and not by the owner's preference for a good or bad animal to sacrifice], and he shall not exchange it [ve-lo yamirennu — וְלֹא יְמִירֶנּוּ; yamirennu — Hifil imperfect of m-w-r, "to exchange/swap"; the same prohibition as in v.10 — once the tenth animal passes under the rod, it is holy and may not be swapped]; and if he does indeed exchange it [ve-im hamor yamirennu — וְאִם-הָמֵר יְמִירֶנּוּ; the same construction as v.10 — the infinitive absolute hamor intensifying the finite verb], then it and its substitute shall both be holy [ve-hayah-hu u-temuratoh yihyeh-qodesh — וְהָיָה-הוּא וּתְמוּרָתֹה יִהְיֶה-קֹּדֶשׁ; identical in structure and outcome to v.10 — attempted substitution doubles the consecration; both animals become holy and neither can be reclaimed] — it shall not be redeemed [lo yig'al — לֹא יִגָּאֵל; the tithed animal and its substitute, once both consecrated through attempted substitution, are permanently holy and cannot be redeemed by a monetary payment; they belong to YHWH irrevocably].

---

**27:34** These are the commandments [elleh ha-mitsvot — אֵלֶּה הַמִּצְוֹת; the closing colophon of the entire book of Leviticus; elleh — "these," the demonstrative referring back to everything commanded in the book; ha-mitsvot — "the commandments/the directives," from ts-w-h, "to command/charge/direct"; mitsvot are the specific divine directives — distinct from chuqqot ("statutes") and mishpatim ("judgments") used in the colophon at 26:46; the final colophon uses the broadest term — all commandments — encompassing the entirety of Leviticus] that the LORD commanded Moshe [asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe — אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת-מֹשֶׁה; tsivvah — Pi'el perfect of ts-w-h, "to command/charge/commission/instruct"; the divine commissioning is complete; everything from the first burnt offering (Lev 1:1) to the final tithe law (Lev 27:32–33) is enclosed within the authoritative word of YHWH to Moshe] for the sons of Yisra'el [el-benei Yisra'el — אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל; the addressees: not Moshe alone but the entire community of Yisra'el; Moshe is the mediating agent, Yisra'el is the recipient community] on Mount Sinai [be-har Sinai — בְּהַר סִינַי; the final three words of the book are the locative signature of the Sinai covenant; be-har Sinai — "on Mount Sinai" — the legislation of Leviticus, from the burnt offerings to the Jubilee to the vow-and-tithe appendix, all originates at Sinai; the mountain-of-God is the source from which Torah flows into the community's life; the book closes where it theologically began — at the mountain of divine revelation; the colophon at 26:46 closed the Holiness Code with the same location; here v.34 closes the entire book with identical authority, bracketing the appendix of chapter 27 within the same Sinai-sanction as everything that preceded it].

---

## Translator's Notes — Leviticus 27

### Key Terminology

**ki yafli neder (27:2)** — "when he makes a special/extraordinary vow" — Hifil of p-l-', "to do extraordinarily." The vow of persons (neder nefashot) in this chapter is not ordinary vow-making but a particularly solemn form of dedication. The root p-l-' underlies pele' ("wonder/marvel") and palil ("judgment/extraordinary determination"). The yafli neder dedicates a person to YHWH's service, perhaps for labor at the sanctuary or through a symbolic monetary payment to the priestly institution.

**be-erkekha / erekh (27:2, 3, etc.)** — "by your valuation / valuation/assessed value" — from '-r-kh, "to arrange, set in order, assess." The erekh is a fixed official rate on a sliding scale, not a market price. It is the sanctuary's standardized system for converting the dedication of persons into monetary contributions. The table (vv.3–7) is the first systematic tariff in the Torah: it creates six demographic categories (age brackets: 0–1 month, 1 month–5 years, 5–20, 20–60, 60+; sex: male/female), assigning fixed sheqel amounts to each. This system served the practical administrative needs of the sanctuary, ensuring that vow-dedications translated into usable resources regardless of individual bargaining.

**sheqel ha-qodesh (27:3, 25)** — "the sheqel of the sanctuary" — the standard weight maintained at the temple/sanctuary against which commercial weights were measured. The sanctuary was the metrology authority: it preserved the canonical standard. 20 gerahs = 1 sheqel; 1 gerah ≈ 0.57 grams; 1 sheqel ≈ 11.4 grams.

**chamishito (27:13, 15, 19, 27, 31)** — "its fifth/one-fifth of it/twenty percent" — the standard redemption surcharge throughout the chapter. The owner may reclaim a consecrated thing by paying the assessed value plus 20%. This surcharge serves multiple functions: (1) it discourages trivial consecration; (2) it compensates the sanctuary for the disruption of an irrevocable dedication being converted back to private use; (3) it acknowledges the priority of YHWH's claim even in the act of redemption.

**cherem (27:21, 28, 29)** — "devoted thing/banned thing/totally dedicated" — from ch-r-m, "to devote/place under sacred ban/consecrate irrevocably." The cherem is the most extreme form of dedication: absolute, irrevocable, irredeemable. What is placed under cherem belongs to YHWH totally. In war contexts (Deut 20:17; Josh 6:17–21) it means total destruction. In priestly contexts (Lev 27:28–29; Num 18:14; Ezek 44:29) it means permanent priestly ownership with no possibility of return to the original owner. The cherem is qodesh-qodashim — most holy — the same level of holiness as the Holy of Holies itself.

**qodesh-qadashim (27:28)** — "most holy/holy of holies" — the construct chain intensifying holiness to its absolute degree. In the Tabernacle/Temple layout, the Holy of Holies (debir) is the innermost sanctum where the Ark of the Covenant stands and only the High Priest enters once a year. In legal contexts, qodesh-qadashim designates things so thoroughly YHWH's that no human ownership or use is possible — grain offerings, sin offerings, guilt offerings, the showbread, and items placed under cherem.

**ma'aser (27:30–33)** — "tithe/tenth" — from '-s-r, "to take/give a tenth." The tithe is not a vow but a standing obligation: the tenth of agricultural produce and every tenth animal belong to YHWH as a fixed divine entitlement. Leviticus 27 is the Torah's formal legal statement of the tithe; it is elaborated in Num 18:21–32 (tithe to the Levites), Deut 14:22–29 (festal tithe and poor-year tithe). The pastoral tithe's counting procedure — animals driven under the rod, every tenth automatically holy — makes the selection mechanical and unmanipulable.

**elleh ha-mitsvot (27:34)** — "these are the commandments" — the final colophon of the book of Leviticus, echoing and surpassing the colophon at 26:46 (which named statutes, judgments, and torot). The final word of Leviticus is be-har Sinai — "on Mount Sinai" — the grounding of all Levitical legislation in the authority of the Sinai covenant.

### Structural Notes

The chapter falls into a tight seven-part structure:
1. **Persons by vow (vv.1–8):** the erekh table (age/sex tariff) with pastoral provision for the poor (v.8)
2. **Animals (vv.9–13):** clean animals (irrevocable, vv.9–10); substitution (v.10); unclean animals (valued by priest + 20% surcharge, vv.11–13)
3. **Houses (vv.14–15):** priestly assessment + 20% surcharge for redemption
4. **Ancestral land (vv.16–21):** valued by seed-capacity per homer; pro-rated to Jubilee; unredeemed land becomes permanently priestly at Jubilee (like cherem)
5. **Purchased land (vv.22–25):** pro-rated to Jubilee; reverts to original owner at Jubilee not to the dedicant
6. **Firstborn / Cherem / Unclean firstborn (vv.26–29):** the absolute categories — what already belongs to YHWH cannot be re-given; the cherem is absolutely irrevocable
7. **Tithes (vv.30–33):** agricultural tithe + 20% surcharge for redemption; pastoral tithe by rod-count — irrevocable, no substitution

The organizing principle is a descending scale from redeemable (persons, houses, purchased land, animals) to conditionally-redeemable-at-cost (ancestral land, unclean animals, agricultural tithe) to absolutely irrevocable (firstborn, cherem, pastoral tithe once counted under the rod).

### LXX Notes (Codex Vaticanus, pp. 113–114)
The LXX (reflected in Vaticanus) translates erekh generally as timê ("valuation/price"), with be-erkekha rendered as "according to your assessment." Cherem is rendered as anathema (ἀνάθεμα) — "devoted offering/consecrated object" — a Greek term that in later Christian usage came to mean "curse/condemnation" but here retains its original sense of "something placed on/up" (ana-tithemi, "to place upon/dedicate"), i.e., dedicated irrevocably to a deity. The LXX rendering of mot yumat (v.29) as thanatô apothaneitai (θανάτω ἀποθανεῖται) — "by death he shall die" — preserves the Hebrew cognate-infinitive construction. The colophon of v.34 in the LXX: autai hai entolai has eneteilato Kyrios tô Môysê pros tous hyious Israêl en tô orei Sina — closely paralleling the Hebrew.

---

*EMET — Leviticus 27 complete. This is the final chapter of Leviticus.*
